coredata.dc:title: Synthesized carbon dots with high N and S content as excellent corrosion inhibitors for copper in sulfuric acid solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Tan, Bochuan
Zhang, Xin
Guo, Lei
Zhang, Shengtao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) had been prepared with content of N and S as high as 17% and 19%, respectively. Combining electrochemical, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and micromorphology techniques to assess the inhibition performance of the N,S-CDs for copper in sulfuric acid solution. The new nanomaterials dispaly excellent effectiveness for copper (99.88%) at the concentration of 50 mg/L in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, this work could provide a new idea for exploring environment-friendly and high efficient corrosion inhitiors for metal protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-CDs
XPS
Electrochemical
Corrosion inhibitors
Copper

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by financially by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878029, and 21676035). Sail plan of Guangdong, China (No. 2015YT02D025).
This work was supported by financially by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( No. 21878029, and 21676035 ). Sail plan of Guangdong, China ( No. 2015YT02D025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the systhesis of N,S-CDs, the left is N,S-CDs dissolved in enthon and the right in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.
(a) TEM image of N,S-CDs, (b) AFM image of N,S-CDs and (c) height profile along the corresponding line.
Fourier Infrared Spectroscopy of thiourea, O-phenylenediamine and N,S-CDs.
(a)Raman spectroscopy of N,S-CDs, XPS information of (b) for (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) S 2p corresponding N,S-CDs.
(a) OCP-time curve and (b) polarization curve of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with different concentrations of N,S-CDs at 298 K.
(a) Nyquist plot, (b) Bode plot of copper electrode dipped in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with various concentrations of N,S-CDs.
the EIS data fitting by equivalent circuit used to fit the blank solution and EIS data containing N, S-CDs.
(a) The polarization curve of copper electrode immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4, (b) the polarization curve of copper electrode immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 containing 50 mg/L N,S-CDs at different temperatures.

(a) Nyquist plot, (b) Bode plot of copper samples dipped in blank solution at different temperatures. (c) Nyquist plot, (d) Bode plot of copper samples dipped in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with 50 mg/L N,S-CDs at different temperatures.

The newly polished electrode surface (a), the copper electrode was immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution presence and absent 50 mg/L N,S-CDs (b, c) for 24 h at 298 K, the corresponding EDS image of copper and the element distribution spectrum (d, e,f).

(a) 3D AFM images of freshly polished copper surface and corresponding height profile, (b) 3D AFM images of the copper dipped in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution and corresponding height profile for 12 h at 298 K. (c) 3D AFM images of the copper dipped in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution presence 50 mg/L N,S-CDs and corresponding height profile for 12 h at 298 K.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the adsorbed surveyed N,S-CDs on the investigated Cu substrates, (b) Raman spectra of the covered studied N,S-CDs on the investigated Cu substrates.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the investigated Cu substrates (b) for (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) S 2p.
The adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism of N,S-CDs on the copper matrix in acid medium is proposed in diagram.
Polarization parameters of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with different concentrations of N,S-CDs at 298 K.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: SD, standard deviation of 3 independent measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical parameters fitted in the EIS results are copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions presence and absent various concentrations of N,S-CDs at 298 K.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: SD, standard deviation of 3 independent measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The parameters of the polarization test results are obtained from the Tafel curve in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution of N,S-CDs with 50 mg/L N,S-CDs at different temperatures.

head.ce:title: Synthesized carbon dots with high N and S content as excellent corrosion inhibitors for copper in sulfuric acid solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) had been prepared with content of N and S as high as 17% and 19%, respectively. Combining electrochemical, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and micromorphology techniques to assess the inhibition performance of the N,S-CDs for copper in sulfuric acid solution. The new nanomaterials dispaly excellent effectiveness for copper (99.88%) at the concentration of 50 mg/L in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, this work could provide a new idea for exploring environment-friendly and high efficient corrosion inhitiors for metal protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper was widely applied to industrial fields owing to its excellent performance that high electrical, good thermal conductivity and corrosion resistance, however, copper can't avoid being corroded in corrosive environments [1–3]. Corrosion lower equipment performance and leads to safety hazards and economic losses, the pickling treatments are usually carried out to remove oxide layers, oily and scales to ensure that the equipment is safe and reliable in metal processing, petroleum engineering and electronic industries etc, sulfuric acid is the most frequently used in the industry owing to convenient to transport and the dosage less than other acids, but it cannot be ignored that corrosion of matrix during chemical cleaning poses a threat to equipment safety and waste of resources [4–6]. Adding inhibitors in corrosive environment is the most effective and economic way among many anticorrosion strategies. Generally speaking, the high coverage of inhibitors on copper surface means higher inhibitive ability, various corrosion inhibitors had been evaluated and applied in the past decades, but most of them are organic compounds with heteroatoms (i.e N, S, O) and unsaturated bond as adsorption active site to form protective barries on copper surface, easily form donor–acceptor interaction with the inhibitor molecules, it can be attributed to the electron withdrawing groups of corrosion inhibitors integrate with the outer layer electrons of copper [3,7–11]. However, most of these evaluated inhibitors are toxicty and environmentally unfriendly even with high anti-corrosion efficiency, most of traditional inhibitors present low water solubility and high cost, which greatly restricts the promotion of industrial applications. Exploring environmental friendly, good watersoluble, and high performance corrosion inhibitors has become increasing attention to protect the environment meeting the requirements of green chemistry.

Carbon nanomaterials were frequently focused on coating in corrosion filed [12–14], no much attempt to explore new marerials applied to inhibitors. Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel carbon-based functional nanomaterials with significant advantages that low toxicity, good water solubility, stable chemical properties, and can be make up from various carbon-based materials [15–19], the characteristics of nanomaterials provides a new idea to explore high performance corrosion inhibitors, it has been received an increasing attention on the application to corrosion inhibitors in field of corrosion protection in recent years [14,20–30]. Compared with the contemporary inhibitors, carbon dots have a larger specific surface area and coordinated chemical properties, and adsorbent and the structure can be designed according to the characteristics of the corrosion inhibitor. According to the adsorption behavior of organic inhibitors on the copper surface, the interaction of heteroatom-rich nanomaterials with copper can be considered as physical adsorption and chemical adsorption [31]. Theoretically, the thickness of the adsorption layer can be grown to nanometer level to greatly improve its anti-corrosion performance. Cui et al studied inhibition performance of NCDs for Q235 carbon steel, the result revealed the significant inhibition efficiency in HCl solution, the protective layer follows the Langmuir adsorption involved both chemisorption and physisorption [28]. Ye used l-histidine and citric acid to prepare N-CDs as green inhibitors, theoretical calculations show that N-CDs can form a dense and orderly strong chemisorption film on metals, the results show that N-CDs exhibit superior corrosion protection to metals [32]. Qiang et al reported that NCDs can effectively protect copper in corrosive sulfuric acid solution, the layer was considered to be the Langmuir adsorption [21]. Cen et al researched anti-corrosion performance of N,S-CDs for carbon steel in 3.5% sodium chloride solution with saturated CO2, these contain N and S functional groups can effectively adhere with Fe surface by chemisorption [29]. However, CDs with larger size and functional groups on the anti-corrosion performance has not been explored for copper, the inhibition mechanism of carbon dots for metal is obscurity and little study.

Inspired by Mani et al developed carbon dots for Cu2+ detection by a simple hydrothermal method [33], N,S-CDs were hoped to become the excellent corrosion inhibitor for copper. Therefore, this work committed to explore the potential corrosion resistance and insight the anticorrosion mechanism of N,S-CDs for copper in sulfuric acid solution. The structure of N,S-CDs probed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), elemental analysis, Raman, atomic force microscopy (AFM), FT-IR and XPS. The anticorrosion ability of N,S-CDs studied using AC impedance, potentiodynamic polarization, and surface analysis of copper achieved from environmental scanning electron microscope (ESEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), atomic force microscopy (AFM). Additionally, the related protection mechanism description was recommended in details.

O-phenylenediamine(o-PD 99%) was purchased from Adamas-beta, sulfuric acid (H2SO4 98 wt%), Thiourea(99 wt%), ethyl alcohol, and pure copper substrates (99.9 wt%, 10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm) were purchased from local supplier, all chemicals were used directly without further majorization and ultrapure water was used during whole the experiment.

The synthesis of N,S-CDs reference the procedure reported by with some improve [26,29,34]. As shown in Fig. 1 , dissolved a mixture of 0.15 g o-PD and 0.15 g thiourea in 30 mL ethanol ultrasonically, then transfer to the solvothermal reaction in 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. The solvent was heated to 180 °C for 16 h, and then naturally cool to room temperature, the reddish brown solution was obtained. The crude production was filtered through the 0.45 μm microporous membrane aim to remove large particles, the filtered solution was purified with dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off~1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted small molecules, ultrapure water for dialysis was replaced many times. The purified solution was further dried through rotary evaporator and further drying under freeze drying, N,S-CDs of brown solid were finally obtained.

The morphology and size distribution of N,S-CDs was observed by TEM (FEI Talos F200S G2) and AFM (MFP 3D BIO, America). The structure information of the N,S-CDs was investigated by FT-IR (Nicolet iS50, America), Raman (LabRAM HR Evolution) and XPS (ESCALAB250Xi, Al Kα 3Xray source). FT-IR spectra were collected from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr tablets, Raman characterization was collected in the spectrum from 100 to 3500 cm−1. XPS spectroscopy was collected spectrometer using an full-range. The Element content of prepared N,S-CDs was identified by Elemental Analyzer (Elementar, Unicube, Germany).

Purchased sulfuric acid diluted with ultrapure water to prepare 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Prepared concentration of N,S-CDs without and with in corrosive solution range from 2 to 50 mg/L as tested electrolyte solution. The working electrode (10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm) was soldered to epoxy, leaving one side area face to the electrolyte along with electrochemical performance test. The copper substrate was abraded with 400–2000 grit emery papers before each measurement, and then ultrasonic cleaning the copper electrode with ultrapure water and ethanol, finally dried under colded natural air flow. Additionally, each experiment uses freshly prepared test solution.

Three-electrode system of CHI 604D electrochemical workstation was used to implement electrochemical measurement, the electrode system was consisted of copper electrode as the working electrode (WE), the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode (RE), and the platinum sheet as the counter electrode (CE). Immersed WE in the solution for a period of time to obtain a stable open circuit potential (E OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested on EOCP in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz under 5 mV sinusoidal potential signal, the polarization measurement was performed at the potential range of −250 mV to +250 mV (versus E OCP) with scanning rate of 2 mV s−1, each experiment was performed three times under the same experimental conditions to ensure good reproducibility.

ESEM (Thermoscientific Quattro S) and AFM were used to observe the surface topography of copper substrate infiltrated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution absence and presence 50 mg/L N, S-CDs at 298 K. The EDS detector combined with ESEM records the corrosion composition on the surface of copper samples.

The element distribution of N,S-CDs was analyzed with an element analyzer, and the results showed that C contains 52 wt%, H was 5 wt%, N was 17 wt%, S was 19 wt%, and the remaining elements was O contains 7%. In particular, the content of N and S are significantly higher than in previous reports, it shows that N and S were successfully mixed into the sample [29].

The morphology and size distribution of the N,S-CDs were analyzed by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the sample dispersed to the ultrasonic carbon film for observation, high-resolution image of TEM clearly shows that the synthesized N,S-CDs has evenly spherical fabric with a size distribution between 10 and 20 nm. Fig. 2c show the AFM 2D image and the homologous height profile of N,S-CDs, the sample was scattered on the mica sheet base for scanning observation, which shows that the height of N,S-CDs similar as TEM result. The size of the synthesized N,S-CDs is different from the uniformity of ordinary CDs, which may be caused by the random polymerization and cleavage reaction of the synthesized nanomaterials [26,35,36], the results of TEM and AFM indicate that N,S-CDs has been successfully achieved.

FT-IR spectroscopy were used to detect the functional group information of o-phenylenediamine, thiourea and obtain N,S-CDs. Analyze the spectra of O-phenylenediamine and thiourea and the absorption peaks of N, S-CDs to confirm their conversion. As shown in Fig. 3 , the sharp obsorption peaks of O-phenylenediamine located at 3364 and 3385 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of NH [21,29,37], 1457 cm−1 cotributite to stretching vibration CN [21], the peak at 3026 cm−1, 1591 cm−1 and 1501 cm−1 contribute to CH and CC illustrate exeist benzene ring structure [14]. In case of spectrum thiourea, the peak at 1468 cm−1, 1092 cm−1 represent the characteristic tensile vibration of NCS. The spectrum for N,S-CDs, the broad absorption peaks at 3500 cm−1 to 2800 cm−1 composed by COOH, OH and NH becase multi-molecule association [37,38]. These hydrophilicity functional groups could explains the stability and good solubility of the carbon dots in aqueous system. The peak at 2064 cm−1 is a typical absorption attributed to the cumulative double bond of NC=S and NC = O, which is the unique difference of N,S-CDs from o-phenylenediamine and thiourea [29,38,39]. Moreover, a new broad peak of 1624 cm−1 attributed to CN, CC and NCO of benzene ring structure vibration [24,39–41]. The peak at 1180 cm−1 can be ascribed to CO and COH groups on the surface of the N,S-CDs [42]. In the heating process, thiourea decomposes at 180℃ degrees and has reducibility, conducive to the realization of intermolecular hybrid reactions [43]. At same time, N,S-CDs were synthesized may be through the polymerization and cleavage of precursor cyclization [33,35–37,40,44–46].

The Raman information was acquired to further confirm the analysis of different carbon status, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. The peak between 700 and 800 cm−1 because of benzene ring deformation and breathing or the isomer of S4 [26,47]. After the solvothermal reaction, two obvious peaks are observed of N,S-CDs, the G band (sp2-hybrid) between 1500 and 1600 cm−1 may be attribute to the NH2 bending deformation and the CC deformation mode of the benzo ring under the vibration of the sp2-bonded CDs, indicating the aromaticity on the surface of the N,S-CDs characteristic [21,26,29,48]. The peaks of o-PD that NH2 scissoring mode, CH in plane bending and CN stretching disappear after the solvothermal reaction is assigned to intermolecular polymerization cracking reactions [26,36]. Combined with the infrared spectrum, it can be inferred that N and S doping has been successfully achieved.

The complete XPS spectrum measurement was used to investigate the bond information of the elements in N, S-CD, the C 1s peak was calibrated to 248.8 eV by advantage due to surface charge binding energy shift, and then use peak software to split peak. Fig. 4b shows high-resolution C1s, N 1s, O1s, S 2p, C 1s spectrum demonstrated three peaks means the differentiated carbon information, the peaks locating at 284.7 eV, 285.1 eV and 285.9 eV attributes to C C, CN/CO and CN components, respectively [26,29,48]. As illustrated in Fig. 4d shows N 1s peak include 399.2 eV and 399.8 eV correspond to NH and CN bands, respectively. The O 1s can be split to 531.3 and 532 eV related to CO, and OH, respectively. For S 2p spectrum show in Fig. 4g, The two peaks associated with S2p1/2 and S2p3/ 2 of the CS and CS are 162.9 and 161.5 eV, respectively, the peaks at 168.4 eV are attributed to CS(O)x C [29,49,50]. Over all, the N,S-CDs have been successfully obtained as expected and the compounds contains plentiful N, O and S groups according to structural analysis, and according to the previous characterization data, the corresponding structure diagram was inferred as shown in Fig. 1.

It is indispensable to observe open circuit potential (OCP) shift in electrolyte liquid for understanding characters of adsorption process of N,S-CDs with copper, meanwhile, gaining the stable interface status for EIS test. Fig. 5 a displays the OCP-time curves for different concentrate of N,S-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K, all the E ocp curves move to negative direction compared to without N,S-CDs, the E ocp lower first and then increase as the concentration increases, especially, the stable E ocp moves to the more positive at higher concentration. The change of E ocp value between blank and and presence of N,S-CDs is less than 85 mV, the adsorption of nanoparticles mainly changes the reaction of the cathode at lower concentration, interestingly, it transform into a joint inhibition of the cathode and the anode as the concentration increases. The reason why E ocp slows down to stable may be attributed to the large size of nanoparticles leading to tardy migration of N, S-CDs in corrosive solution [26].

The potentiodynamic polarization was to study the inhibitory ability of N,S-CDs for copper corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as shown in Fig. 5b. Correlation parameters are epitomized in Table 1 including the corrosion current density (i corr), corrosion potential (E corr), anode slope (β a) and cathode Tafel slope (β c) and suppression efficiency (η) are derived from the polarization curve by classical extrapolation. The corresponding η value is calculated as following formula

η % =
i
corr , 0
-
i
corr
i
corr , 0
× 100 %
(1)where i corr,0 and i corr are the current densities of the unprotected and protected copper electrodes, respectively. It is observed that the addition of N,S-CDs causes negative shift of the copper corrosion potential and significant decrease in the current density of the anode and cathode compared with the blank solution. In addition, the slope of the cathode and the slope of the anode are both rapidly change with the increasing concetration of N,S-CDs, the corrosion current density continues to decrease, which means that both the anode (copper dissolution) and the cathode reaction (oxygen reduction) are suppressed, especially the cathode change is more obvious. Additionally, β c of slope of Tafel curve at 2 mg/L significantly drop down and obviously distinguish with blank, thus the corrosion resistance behavior mainly depends on reducing the current density, the value of η range from 78.6% at 2 mg/L to 98.4% at 50 mg/L of N,S-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, this may be due to the adsorption of different sizes of N,S-CDs on the copper surface, which becomes denser due to the accumulation effect of stones and sand, and blocking the active sites on copper electrode surface. The desorption behavior on the anode branch was easily observed, the more positive desorption voltage along with lower rate means more stable adsorption as the increased concentration. These results indicate that the N,S-CDs can be considered as a mixed-type corrosion inhibitor with significantly inhibiting the cathodic process of copper [51,52].

EIS aims to obtain information about the surface characteristics of the copper sample and the dynamics of the electrode process in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with the absence and presence of the different concentration N,S-CDs at 298 K as shown in Fig. 6 , respectively. The spectrum for the non-inhibited solution shows a convex capacitive semicircle at high frequencies and trailing straight lines at low frequencies. Anode reaction leads to metal dissolution as copper has a higher potential than hydrogen ions, while hydrogen evolution and oxygen reduction occured at the cathode [21]. Additionally, the curves of all solution with N,S-CDs showing capacitive semicircle at high frequency and low frequency region, Warburg impedance was suppressed at low frequency maybe owing to the protective layer slow down the mass transfer rate of oxygen.

Two obvious time constant are caused by the relaxation process shown in the Bode diagram (Fig. 6b and Fig. 8d), the fitting circuit selects the equivalent circuit basis of the correspondence. The EIS experimental data was fitted by the equivalent circuit as shown in Fig. 7
, and constant phase element (CPE) is more suitable for EIS data fitting than pure capacitors, and n is the phase shift, which is the degree of surface unevenness (when n = 1 represent pure capacitor, n = 0.5 represent Warburg impedance, n = −1 represent inductance, n = 0 represent resistor). Where Rs represent the solution resistance, Rf represent the film resistance of the corrosion products film, Cf reflects the capacitance response of the corrosion product film, and Nf represent the modulus of the dispersion effect corresponding to the corrosion product film. C dl is the double layer capacitor related to constant phase angle element, Ndl is the coefficient of the dispersion effect related to the electrode and the solution, R ct is the charge transfer resistance of the corrosion reaction [52].

The impedance of CPE (ZCPE) can be expressed as follows, where ωmax = 2πf max, f max is the maximum frequency, j is the imaginary root, Y 0 is the amplitude of the CPE, the capacitance value of C dl can be expressed according to the following formula [51,53].
(2)
Z
CPE
=
Y
0
- 1
( j
ω
max
)
- n
(3)
C
dl
=
Y 0
( ω )
n - 1
=
Y 0
( 2 π
f
Z
max
)
n - 1

The inhibition efficiency of N,S-CDs for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution is calculated by the polarization resistance Rp as following formulas, where Rp and Rp,0 represent the sum of Rct and Rf, the value of η according to the EIS data were calculated as following equation [3]
(4)
R P
=
R f
+
R
ct
(5)
η % =
R P
-
R
P , 0
R P
× 100 %
W is the Warburg resistance caused by insufficient adsorption on active site of the copper electrode surface, χ2 means the fitting error represents the deviation between experimental data and the fitted (less than 10−3), Table 3
lists the value of the electrochemical parameters of the corresponding fitting results.

The Nyquist diagram of copper electrode without and with N,S-CDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as show in Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b, the diameter of loop remarkably increases with continuously augment of N,S-CDs concentration. The capacitive arc in the high-frequency region is attributed to the capacitive response of the protective layer adsorbed on the electrode surface, and the lack of capacitive arc in the low-frequency region is due to the protective film preventing the mass transfer process, the Warburg resistance disappears with the N,S-CDs existence, this reduces the rate about oxygen diffuses to the copper electrode. In addition, the complexes of N,S-CDs with copper ions to form a stable potential barrier, further reducing the solute diffusion and charge transfer process. This indicated that the good adsorption of corrosion inhibitors prevents the diffusion of dissolved oxygen to the copper electrode surface and inhibits the dissolution of copper.

Combining Bode diagram analysis and fitting of the EIS data in solution with 50 mg/L N,S-CDs, the digital of low-frequency impedance surged to two orders of magnitude compared with the blank solution. The frequency range of the maximum phase angle increased with the increasing concentration of N,S-CDs, at the same time, maintaining a larger phase angle in a wider frequency range. It is noticed that the slope of the linear relationship between log|Z| and logf is closer to − 1, and the phase angle close to −90° with the increases concentration of N,S-CDs, which shows typical capacitive behavior. This result revealed that N,S-CDs can be effectively adsorbed on the copper surface to form well stable protective film.

For protected copper by N,S-CDs, the fitted value of the AC impedance data can be seen to be much higher than the unprotected in Table 2, which indicates that the addition of the inhibitors greatly reduces corrosion rate of copper in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. As shown the Table 2, the reduce of R s may be due to the particles accelerate the migration rate of overall particle in the solution. R f value increase while C f decrease along with the concentration addtion, it suggest that the denser adsorption layer increased film hindrance shows weakened capacitive behavior, which could be owing to the copper surface cover by these uniformly sized nanoparticles. Furtherly, it is clearly that R ct dominate the major position with value of Rct are soaring, whereas the C dl decrease with the increasing concentration, the capacitive behavior of double layer with copper occupied major position, this phenomenon may be attribute to the size effect of nano particles. It can be speculated that N,S-CDs as active adsorbents to form a protective layer on surface of the copper electrode, the nanomaterials gradually replace H2O molecules on the interface of copper and solution, which slows down the electron transfer process that copper are corroded, accompanied by an increase in the resistance becuase increased film thickness, it is beneficial to prevent oxygen and water molecules from contacting the copper surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. As a result, the value of η increases with the N,S-CDs concentration addtion, from 96.3 at 2 mg/L to 99.83% at 50 mg/L at 298 K. The excellent anti-corrosion performance is well consistent with polarization results, it shows that the N,S-CDs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper in the corrosive sulfuric acid solution.

Impedance and polarization measurement to explore the anti-corrosion performance of N,S-CDs under different temperature conditions. Fig. 8 shows that the recorded polarization curve was used to analyze the corrosion of copper electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without of 50 mg/L N,S-CDs. The parameters obtained are calculated in Table 3, the curve shifts to a higher current density in both blank solution and the presence of N,S-CDs along with the temperature increasing. Corrosion rate of copper were accelerated with the increase of temperature, the intensification of the corrosion process leads to the increase of the i corr value in both blank and the presence of N,S-CDs, the rising temperature exacerbate the thermal movement of the nanoparticles, making the adsorption layer unstable. Compared with the blank solution, when adding N,S-CDs into 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at different temperature, the icorr value is significantly reduced means that N,S-CDs can greatly protect copper in sulfuric acid solution. The corresponding Nyquist diagram of unprotected and protected copper electrodes as shown in Fig. 9 a and c, Obviously weakened capacitive behavior exists means that there was physically adsorbed diffusion on the surface. Fig. 9b and d displayed the Bode diagram, the phase angle and capacitance characteristics become significantly lower at higher temperatures, this indicates that the higher temperature accelerates the particle movement rate, making the electrode N,S-CDs particles and corrosion products on the surface spread faster, which reduces the corrosion resistance. Fortunately, even though the temperature rise will accelerate the corrosion, the value of η decreased from 98.4% at 298 K to 89.4% at 318 K, N, S-CDs still exerts excellent inhibition performance.

The current SEM image and the corresponding EDS spectrum of a copper sample soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium for 24 h at 298 K with and without 50 mg/L N,S-CDs. The surface of all observed copper samples was smoothed by 2000, 3000, 5000, 7000 emery papers. Compared with the new polishing (Fig. 10 a), the unprotected copper substrate was immersed in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution (Fig. 10b), it can be clearly seen that the copper has a large amount of corrosion crack. The copper surface protected by N,S-CDs (Fig. 10c) displayed basically the same texture with some shallow pits compared to the newly polished and smoother than the corrosion of copper in the blank solution, this result indicates that N,S-CDs well protect copper from corrosion in sulfuric acid solution. Corresponding EDS spectroscopy aims to dispaly the elemental composition of corrosion products and further confirm the N,S-CDs successfully adsorbed on the copper surface. Compared with the unprotected copper sample, the distribution density of C and N increase markedly, while the O decreases greatly (Fig. 10d and e), it may attribute to the N,S-CDs reduced copper contact with oxygen that dissolve in the solution, the insignificant change of S may be due to the presence of SO2-4 in the solution. The C and N elements are spontaneously and uniformly dispersed on the copper substrate, simultaneously, self-aggregation of N,S-CDs was observed in the enlarged surface morphology as shown in Fig. 10f, it demonstrate that the added N,S-CDs are successfully adsorbed on the copper.

AFM was widely applied to investigate the surface morphology on the corrosion field. The AFM 3D images displayed the surface roughness of the copper specimens soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without of 50 mg/L N,S-CDs for 12 h at 298 K as illustrated in Fig. 11 respectively. The surface treatment of all copper samples is the same as the SEM test. Compared with the newly polished surface (Fig. 11a), the copper surface in the blank solution obviously has more serious pits and porous. The corrosion height profile of the copper sample surface shows a large fluctuation of about 250 nm (Fig. 11b). While the inhibitor adding into the corrosive solution, the surface of copper sample (Fig. 11c) is relatively flat and and small fluctuations in the height within 80 nm can be noticed, which indicates that the N,S-CDs were significant reduced the corrosion rate of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Moreover, by introducing N,S-CDs, the average roughness (Ra) was reduced from 40.893 nm (corroded surface) to 15.363 nm (protected surface). These findings further confirm that the presence of N,S-CDs can effectively protect copper from corrosion by sulfuric acid solution.

FT-IR spectroscopic showing in Fig. 12 a that the bands in the region of 400–4000 cm−1 are produced by the N,S-CDs modified Cu specimens, the broad range of 3300–2900 cm−1 are ascribed to CH, COOH and OH groups of the N,S-CDs, as compared with information of N,S-CDs, the characteristic peak of the CN(1610 cm−1), NCS (2064 cm−1) and CN (1459 cm−1) bonding were not obvious, the peak at 1100 cm−1 reveals the presence of sulfate.

Raman spectra of the protected Cu base are displayed in Fig. 12b, the peaks locating in 2850–3200 cm−1 are derived from the vibration of CH of N,S-CDs, the strong peak between 1400 and 1650 cm−1 attribute to the aromatic ring vibration of graphite structure of the N,S-CDs. In addition, no conspicuous chemical bond extension between C and N atoms in the range of 960–1280 cm−1 was found [7,49]. While the faint peak of N:Cu can be found in the range of 390–570 cm−1, this situation maybe because the lots of N,S-CDs adsorbed on the copper surface, and the peak information was masked. The results further reflected the chemical interactions between the N,S-CDs and the Cu sample, indicating that chemical chelating bonds may be formed between N,S-CDs and Cu ions.

The XPS spectra of the investigative copper specimen modified with N,S-CDs show the peaks corresponding to Cu 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively. The chemical composition of the corrosion product was obtained by XPS spectroscopy [54], the content of C element and N element was roughly same as the content of the prepared sample, while the S element and O element could not be accurately detected due to the influence of sulfate radicals, it was further verified that the carbon dots were successfully synthesized.

In the XPS spectrum of Cu 2p, the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/ 2 peaks can be observed at 932.5 eV and 952.3 eV, respectively, that could be attributed to Cu(I), no satellite peaks appeared at approximate 943 eV and 963 eV indicating that no Cu(II) species were formed [55]. As show in Fig. 13 c, the peak at 284.8 eV corresponds to the CC bond, no characteristic peak of CN bond are unearthed in the protected copper specimens [49]. The spectra for N 1s showing in Fig. 13d, the different chemical N 1s are located at binding energies of 401.4 eV are derived from the N:Cu component. The the O 1s spectrum the peaks at approximate 531.2 eV correspond to Cu2O, which are engendered by the oxidation of copper and dissociate oxygen in sulfuric acid solution, the semaphore about Cu2O spectra in O 1s further account that corrosion process occured at the cuprous step [7]. For S 2p spectrum show in Fig. 13f, the new peaks at 162.5 eV attributed to Cu-S [21], this indicates that N,S-CDs can be effectively adsorbed on the copper surface through the chemical bonds.

The binding energy indicates that the interaction between N,S-CDs and the investigated copper sample are due to the chemical coordination between the polar groups of N,S-CDs and Cu(I). The results show that the corrosion remains in the process of transferring Cu(0) to Cu(I) during the copper immersion process. In addition, no outcrop of Cu(II) species were on the investigated copper surface, the results reflect that N,S-CDs can protect copper well based on chemical adsorption. Combined with the electrochemical test results, the possible adsorption mechanism was inferred that much polar groups on the structure of N,S-CDs nanoparticles are first combined with metal active sites, based on the quantum size effect of nanomaterials, they are finally formed through self-aggregation growth, and the protective layer can effectively muffle copper corrosion in sulfuric acid solution.

In this study, N,S-CDs species was as corrosion inhibitor and firstly applied to protect copper in sulfuric acid solution environment. It is necessary to figure out the interaction between the copper and N,S-CDs for clarifying the anticorrosion mechanism. The proofs that electrochemical performance and micro morphology strongly reveal the adsorption film formed on copper surface in acid medium. The cathodic oxygen reduction reaction equation is given as follows according to reports [56].
O 2
+ 4
H
+
+ 4
e
-
→ 2
H 2
O

The anodic dissolution process of copper in H2SO4 mediums as reported as follows [2,21]
Cu ↔
Cu
ads
+
+
e
-
( f a s t )
Cu
ads
+
↔
Cu
2 +
+
e
-
( s l o w )

Even if the process that Cu transform to Cu + ads is fast but Cu + ads does not easy diffuse into 0.5 M H2SO4 corrosive solution, the slow transformation that Cu + ads lose electrons and turn into soluble Cu2+ and then migrate into the solution, generally speaking, the slow process is rate-determining step. The adsorption study illustrate the interaction of the N,S-CDs and copper electrode is mainly chemical adsorption, the chemisorption may be based on covalent bonds that is the donor-acceptor interaction between heteroatoms (N, S, O) and copper. The empty orbitals of the N and S groups that rich in N,S-CDs with withdrawing electrons tendency, while the external orbital electrons of Cu atoms have a tendency to donate electrons, these lone pairs of electrons combine with the empty d orbitals of copper to form stable coordination bonds with lower chemical potential [2,56]. The possible compound formation mechanism is as follows
Cu
ads
+
+ x N , S - C D s ↔
C u
N , S - C D s
x
ads
+
Cu + x N , S - C D s ↔ [ C u -
( N , S - C D s )
x
]
Cu
2 +
+ x N , S - C D s ↔
[ C u
( N , S - C D s )
x
]
2 +
where x represents the number of N,S-CDs nanoparticles adsorbed on a Cu atom, the [Cu (N,S-CDs)x] + ads and [Cu-(N,S-CDs)x] as the main chelated products are chemically adsorbed on the copper surface to form strong protective barries. While the [Cu-(N,S-CDs)x]2+ represents that N,S-CDs can absorb the Cu2+ to form chelate and slow its diffusion [57,58]. In this case (Fig. 14 ), the N,S-CDs make the adsorption layer more compactly packed becuase its different particle sizes, so that the adsorption layer prevents the contact between water and the Cu surface, therefore, the tight N,S-CDs barrier layer can isolate the active sites on copper surface from the corrosive environment and slow down the corrosion rate.

A novel high content of N and S of carbon dots were designed and successfully synthesized, and it was firstly used as the corrosion inhibition for copper in H2SO4 solution. Based on systematic experiment and theoretical verification, the significant conclusions are summarized as follows.
(1)
The prepared N,S-CDs was successed synthesised from O-phenylenediamine and thiourea, and the nanoparticle size is evenly distributed in 10–20 nm, the structure information was given by elemental analysis, FIRT, Raman, XPS that illustrate the N,S-CDs contain abundant N, S groups.
(2)
The tafel results show that N,S-CDs can be considered as an excellent mixed inhibitor, and the η value increases along with the increase of N,S-CDs concentration. The EIS results are consistent with the polarization measurement, it is inferred that a stable N,S-CDs adsorption layer can be formed on the copper surface to effectively inhibit copper corrosion.
(3)
The polarization curve was used to study the influence of temperature on the anti-corrosion performance of N, S-CDs. Although the temperature rise will accelerate the corrosion process, the N, S-CDs can still protect copper well in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298–318 K.
(4)
Observation of surface morphology and corresponding EDS analysis offered a strong support for the electrochemical results, and further confirmed that N,S-CDs can effectively protect copper from corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.
(5)
Combining experimental data and theoretical research, it can be considered that the interaction between the protective layer of N,S-CDs with copper is mainly chemical adsorption.

Yu Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Software, Writing - original draft. Bochuan Tan: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xin Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Lei Guo: Writing - review & editing. Shengtao Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by financially by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878029, and 21676035). Sail plan of Guangdong, China (No. 2015YT02D025).
